JANEL CORP SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Janel Corporation, a diversified company operating in the Logistics, Life Sciences, and Manufacturing sectors, has released its 2025 10-K report. The report highlights significant financial growth, strategic acquisitions, and operational achievements, while also addressing the challenges and risks faced by the company.

Financial Highlights

Janel Corporation reported robust financial performance for fiscal 2025:

  • Revenues: $207.443 million, an increase of 13.2% from fiscal 2024, primarily due to growth in the Life Sciences and Logistics segments.
  • Gross Profit: $64.993 million, reflecting a 15.6% increase from fiscal 2024, driven by organic growth and contributions from acquisitions.
  • Income from Operations: $6.434 million, up from $3.758 million in fiscal 2024, due to increased profits in the Logistics segment.
  • Net Income: $5.657 million, a significant increase from $551 thousand in fiscal 2024, largely due to higher profits in operating segments and non-cash gains from marketable securities.
  • Net Income Per Share: $4.69 per diluted share, compared to $0.45 per diluted share in fiscal 2024, reflecting improved profitability.

Business Highlights

Janel Corporation operates in three primary business segments, each contributing uniquely to the company's overall operations and growth strategy:

  • Logistics Segment: A non-asset-based provider of cargo transportation logistics management services, including freight forwarding, customs brokerage, and warehousing. It operates out of 28 full-service locations in the U.S. and maintains a network of independent agent relationships globally. The segment handled approximately 148,000 individual shipments in fiscal 2025, with significant revenue contributions from trucking, ocean freight, and air freight services.
  • Life Sciences Segment: Manufactures and distributes antibodies and research and diagnostic reagents. It operates from a 40-acre facility in Davis, California, and three other leased locations in the U.S. and Australia.
  • Manufacturing Segment: Represented by Indco, Inc., produces mixing equipment for various industries. It operates from a facility in New Albany, Indiana, and serves a diverse customer base.

Janel completed acquisitions in both the Logistics and Life Sciences segments in fiscal 2025, including a majority stake in Interlog USA, Inc. and Biosensis Pty Ltd, enhancing its operational capabilities and market reach.

Looking ahead, Janel plans to continue expanding its Logistics segment through additional branch offices, new revenue streams, and acquisitions. The Life Sciences and Manufacturing segments aim to introduce new product lines and enhance distribution efforts.

Strategic Initiatives

Janel Corporation's strategic initiatives focus on growth through organic expansion and acquisitions within its existing segments:

  • Acquisitions: In 2025, Janel completed acquisitions of Biosensis in the Life Sciences segment and Interlog in the Logistics segment to enhance its market position and operational capabilities.
  • Capital Management: The company managed its capital through various credit facilities, including the Santander Facility for the Logistics segment and the First Merchants Credit Agreement for the Life Sciences and Manufacturing segments. Janel reduced its outstanding borrowings under the Santander Facility to $0 by September 30, 2025, from $19,313 the previous year, reflecting improved cash management. The company also increased its cash and cash equivalents significantly by $8,958 to $12,040 as of September 30, 2025.

Janel plans to continue its growth strategy by expanding its Logistics segment through additional branch offices, new revenue streams, and acquisitions. The Life Sciences and Manufacturing segments are expected to introduce new product lines and expand distribution. The company is focused on maintaining adequate financing to support these strategic initiatives and may seek to enter new business segments through acquisitions.

Challenges and Risks

Janel Corporation faces several challenges and risks as it pursues its growth strategy:

  • Integration of Acquisitions: The company faces challenges in integrating acquired businesses and realizing expected benefits, which could lead to potential operational disruptions and the need for additional management resources.
  • Market and Competitive Risks: Janel may face competition from entities with greater resources, impacting its ability to negotiate favorable acquisition terms. The company is also exposed to market risks such as changes in U.S. trade policies and tariffs, which could affect the Logistics segment.
  • Operational Risks: The Manufacturing segment faces risks related to raw material availability and cost fluctuations. Additionally, reliance on third-party carriers in the Logistics segment could adversely affect operations.
  • Regulatory Compliance: The Logistics segment is subject to extensive regulation, including customs brokerage and transportation security standards, which influence its operational practices.
  • Environmental and Sustainability Risks: Climate change and sustainability issues may lead to increased costs and operational disruptions.

Management is focused on supporting subsidiaries' growth, allocating capital efficiently, and retaining talent to address these challenges. However, the company acknowledges the inherent risks in its acquisition strategy and the potential impact on its financial condition.

SEC Filing: JANEL CORP [ JANL ] - 10-K - Dec. 05, 2025

data.title: Predictmedix AI Announces Closing of Final Tranche of Non-Brokered Private Placement
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - December 5, 2025) - Predictmedix AI Inc. (OTC Pink: PMEDF) (FSE: 3QP0) ("Predictmedix" or the "Company"), an emerging leader in AI-powered health and safety technology, is pleased to announce that it has successfully closed the final tranche of its non-brokered private placement (the "Offering").

In this final tranche, the Company issued 3,050,000 units ("Units") at a price of $0.05 per Unit, for gross proceeds of $152,500, bringing the total capital raised across all tranches of the Offering to $612,650.

The Company issued 3,050,000 units ("Units") at a price of $0.05 per Unit, raising $152,500 in this tranche. Each Unit consists of one common share of Predictmedix and one half of one common share purchase warrant (each, a "Warrant"). Each Warrant will entitle the holder thereof to purchase one half Common Share of the Company at a price of C$0.15 for a period of 36 months following the closing date of the Offering. If, following four months and a day after the Closing Date, the volume weighted average price of the Common Shares on the CSE is equal to or greater than $0.30 for any 10 consecutive trading days, the Corporation may, upon providing written notice to the holders of Warrants, accelerate the expiry date of the Warrants to the date that is 30 days following the date of such written notice.

"We are pleased to complete this financing with a select group of strategic participants who align closely with our long-term vision," said Dr. Rahul Kushwah, COO of Predictmedix AI. "In order to maintain share-structure integrity and avoid unnecessary dilution for our long-term investors, we opted to accept a reduced amount and take a disciplined, value-focused approach to the raise. Our priority was to welcome investors who bring meaningful strategic contributions through their expertise and networks, rather than simply maximizing capital. With this measured financing now concluded, we are moving ahead with clarity and strong momentum as we continue advancing our initiatives across key markets."

All securities issued in connection with the private placement are subject to a statutory hold period of four months and one day from the date of issuance.

Details of the company's recent product validations, pilot programs, and strategic partnerships can be found in earlier press releases available at: https://predictmedix.com/press-releases/

About Predictmedix AI Inc.Predictmedix AI Inc. (OTC Pink: PMEDF) (FSE: 3QP0) is an emerging provider of rapid health screening and remote patient care solutions globally. The Company's Smarthealth AI stations – powered by a proprietary artificial intelligence (AI) – use multispectral cameras to analyze physiological data patterns and predict a variety of health issues including 19 physiological vital parameters, impairment by drugs or alcohol, fatigue, or various mental illnesses. Predictmedix AI's proprietary remote patient care platform empowers medical professionals with a suite of AI-powered tools to improve patient health outcomes. To learn more, please visit our website at www.Predictmedix.com or follow us on Twitter, Instagram or LinkedIn.

data.title: Fitch Revises Hungary's Outlook to Negative; Affirms at 'BBB'
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Frankfurt am Main-05 December 2025:

Fitch Ratings has revised the Outlook on Hungary's Long-Term Foreign-Currency Issuer Default Rating (IDR) to Negative from Stable and affirmed the IDR at 'BBB'.

A full list of rating actions is at the end of this rating action commentary.

Key Rating Drivers

The revision of the Outlook on Hungary's IDRs reflects the following key rating drivers and their relative weights:

High

Large Government Deficits: Fitch has significantly worsened its projections for public finances, reflecting new measures as 2026 parliamentary elections approach. We project the fiscal deficit will widen from 5% of GDP in 2025 to 5.6% in 2026, significantly above our June review projections, the government's initial targets (3.7% in 2025-2026) and the current forecast 'BBB' median of 3%.

The cumulative cost of fiscal easing measures, most of which are permanent, is estimated at 0.3% of GDP in 2025 and 2.1% in 2026. The government has extended sectoral windfall profit taxes into 2026, while raising the tax rate and tightening deductibility rules for banks. However, Fitch considers that additional ad-hoc fiscal easing measures in early-2026 cannot be ruled out.

Uncertain Fiscal Consolidation Path: Fitch considers that frequent revisions to government's targets have weakened policy predictability and increased fiscal risks. Contrary to the government's earlier commitment to a balanced primary position consistent with a gradual reduction in public debt/GDP, we expect the primary deficit to widen to 0.4% of GDP in 2025 and 1.4% in 2026, placing debt on an upward trajectory.

The weaker starting position will increase the challenges for Hungary to reverse the projected fiscal deterioration after the elections. Fitch expects the headline deficit to narrow to 5% in 2027 reflecting the modest growth outlook, and the risk that political considerations could limit the space to increase revenues or reduce generous social support measures after the elections. Hungary plans to maintain defence spending at 2% of GDP in 2025-2026 (up from 1.1% in 2021, Eurostat methodology), but the activation of the national escape clause under the EU Stability and Growth Pact has provided additional fiscal flexibility of around 1% of GDP, which will be allocated to non-defence spending.

Rising Debt: Fitch forecasts government debt/GDP will gradually increase to 74.6% by end-2027, from 73.5% in 2024, above the projected 'BBB' median of 61.5% and our June baseline (when we expected debt to decline) reflecting the combination of wider deficits, the projected modest growth outlook and gradual exchange rate depreciation. The repricing of retail securities underpins the projected reduction in interest/revenue to 10.7% in 2025, above the projected 9.2% for the 'BBB' median but lower than the peak at 11.6% in 2024, despite tighter domestic monetary policy conditions and high global uncertainty.

Medium

Weak Economic Growth: Fitch forecasts real GDP growth at just 0.3% in 2025, meaning the 2023-2025 average will be 0%. Fixed investment will contract for the third consecutive year amid tight monetary policy, lower public capex partly due to reduced EU fund inflows, and high uncertainty. Private consumption growth will ease in 2025 as real wage growth slows and consumer sentiment remains weak. We expect real GDP growth to rise to 2.3% in 2026 as private consumption picks up owing to tax cuts, and investment gradually returns. Improved external demand and new automotive and battery production capacity will boost exports and support growth at 2.6% in 2027.

Hungary's 'BBB' IDRs also reflect the following key rating drivers:

Credit Fundamentals: Hungary's ratings are supported by strong structural indicators relative to 'BBB' peers, including GDP per capita well above the peer median. These strengths are balanced against high public debt relative to peers, a record of unorthodox economic policies, the economy's high exposure to external shocks, and a worsening of governance indicators in recent years to closer to the 'BBB' median. Hungary continues to rely on Russian energy imports, leaving it exposed to potential supply disruptions and price changes. Near-term oil supply risks have been mitigated by a one-year exemption from US sanctions on Russia's Lukoil.

2026 Elections: Parliamentary elections are due by mid-April 2026, with most polls showing the opposition TISZA party in the lead since mid-2025. However, the election result is uncertain, and Hungary's electoral system means polling does not map directly to seats in parliament. The election results could have implications for economic policy and Hungary's external relations.

Inflationary Pressures; Tight Monetary Policy: Headline inflation eased to 4.3% in October from 5.6% in February, due to the implementation of price restrictions and currency appreciation. We expect the price measures to be gradually withdrawn from mid-2026, and see inflation on average at 4.5% in 2025, 3.5% in 2026 and 4.4% in 2027, above the projected peer median of 2.9%. Given the risks of inflation remaining above the central bank's target over the medium term and a commitment to positive real interest rates, we see the key rate at 6% at end-2026 (currently 6.5%).

Reduced External Vulnerabilities: We expect the current account surplus (CAS) to widen to 1.8% of GDP in 2025, from 1.6% in 2024. Stronger external demand and new production facilities will boost exports in 2026-2027 but will be partially offset by increased import activity stemming from the projected economic recovery. We expect the CAS to average 2.1% in 2026-2027, above the 'BBB' peer median of -0.6%. The reserves-to-current external payments are forecast to increase to 3.4 months in 2025-2027, from 2.8 months in 2024, the strongest in a decade, but still below the projected 'BBB' median of 5.5 months.

Strong House Price Growth: The government launched a new subsidised lending programme for first-time homebuyers (Home Start Program) in September 2025, which will boost demand for housing and likely contribute to a further increase in house prices (17.9% yoy in 2Q25). reflecting strong demand and scarce supply. The central bank estimates an 18.8% overvaluation of dwellings compared to fundamentals, but risks are contained as the banking system remains stable, underpinned by a high average total capital ratio (20.6% in 1H25), strong profitability (return on equity at 16.1% in 1H25) and liquidity buffers.

ESG - Governance: Hungary has an ESG Relevance Score (RS) of '5[+]' for both Political Stability and Rights and for the Rule of Law, Institutional and Regulatory Quality and Control of Corruption. These scores reflect the high weight that the World Bank Governance Indicators (WBGI) have in our proprietary Sovereign Rating Model (SRM). Hungary has a medium WBGI ranking at 63rd percentile reflecting solid institutions, backed by EU membership, balanced with concerns over the weakening of the rule of law and a moderate level of corruption.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

-Public Finances: A sustained increase in general government/GDP over the medium-term, for example, due to the lack of a credible fiscal consolidation strategy.

- Macro: Domestic political or policy developments that have adverse economic and financial market impacts.

-Macro: Prolonged weak economic growth, for example, due to structural economic challenges or lower foreign direct investment.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

-Public Finances: A sustained decline in general government debt/GDP over the medium-term, for example, due to faster than projected fiscal consolidation.

-Macro/Structural: An improved institutional environment and policymaking that support a strengthening of medium-term growth.

Sovereign Rating Model (SRM) and Qualitative Overlay (QO)

Fitch's proprietary SRM assigns Hungary a score equivalent to a rating of 'BBB' on the Long-Term Foreign-Currency (LT FC) IDR scale.

Fitch's sovereign rating committee did not adjust the output from the SRM to arrive at the final LT FC IDR.

Fitch's SRM is the agency's proprietary multiple regression rating model that employs 18 variables based on three-year centred averages, including one year of forecasts, to produce a score equivalent to a LT FC IDR. Fitch's QO is a forward-looking qualitative framework designed to allow for adjustment to the SRM output to assign the final rating, reflecting factors within our criteria that are not fully quantifiable and/or not fully reflected in the SRM.

Debt Instruments: Key Rating Drivers

Senior Unsecured Debt Equalised: The senior unsecured long-term debt ratings are equalised with the applicable Long-Term IDR, as Fitch assumes recoveries will be 'average' when the sovereign's Long-Term IDR is 'BB-' and above. No Recovery Ratings are assigned at this rating level. The senior unsecured short-term debt ratings are equalised with the applicable Short-Term IDR.

Country Ceiling

The Country Ceiling for Hungary is 'A', three notches above the LT FC IDR. This reflects very strong constraints and incentives, relative to the IDR, against capital or exchange controls being imposed that would prevent or significantly impede the private sector from converting local currency into foreign currency and transferring the proceeds to non-resident creditors to service debt payments.

Fitch's Country Ceiling Model produced a starting point uplift of +3 notches above the IDR. Fitch's rating committee did not apply a qualitative adjustment to the model result.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. Climate Vulnerability Signals

The results of our Climate.VS screener did not indicate an elevated risk for Hungary.

ESG Considerations

Hungary has an ESG Relevance Score of '5[+]' for Political Stability and Rights as WBGI have the highest weight in Fitch's SRM and are therefore highly relevant to the rating and a key rating driver with a high weight. As Hungary has a percentile rank above 50 for the respective Governance Indicator, this has a positive impact on the credit profile.

Hungary has an ESG Relevance Score of '5[+]' for Rule of Law, Institutional & Regulatory Quality and Control of Corruption as WBGI have the highest weight in Fitch's SRM and are therefore highly relevant to the rating and are a key rating driver with a high weight. As Hungary has a percentile rank above 50 for the respective Governance Indicators, this has a positive impact on the credit profile.

Hungary has an ESG Relevance Score of '4[+]' for Human Rights and Political Freedoms as the Voice and Accountability pillar of the WBGI is relevant to the rating and a rating driver. As Hungary has a percentile rank above 50 for the respective Governance Indicator, this has a positive impact on the credit profile.

Hungary has an ESG Relevance Score of '4[+]' for Creditor Rights as willingness to service and repay debt is relevant to the rating and is a rating driver for Hungary, as for all sovereigns. As Hungary has a record of 20+ years without a restructuring of public debt as captured in our SRM variable, this has a positive impact on the credit profile.

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

data.title: Fitch Affirms Austria at 'AA'; Outlook Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Frankfurt am Main-05 December 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed Austria's Long-Term Foreign-Currency Issuer Default Rating (IDR) at 'AA'. The Outlook is Stable.

A full list of rating actions is at the end of this rating action commentary.

Key Rating Drivers

Credit Fundamentals: Austria's rating is supported by a diversified and wealthy economy, the reserve currency status of the euro, governance indicators that are in line with the median of 'AA' rated peers, and sound external finances. This is balanced against a significantly higher government debt ratio relative to the median ratios of both 'AA' and 'AAA' category peers, and low medium-term growth potential.

Large Budget Deficit, Slow Consolidation: Austria's coalition government adopted a "double budget" on 18 June 2025, presenting a two-year fiscal plan that targets general government fiscal deficits of 4.5% in 2025 and 4.2% in 2026, reflecting net consolidation measures of EUR6.4 billion and EUR8.7 billion, respectively. We expect the government to achieve these target fiscal deficits in 2025 and 2026. However, this represents a slight deterioration from our June projections, by 0.2pp in 2025 and 0.3pp in 2026, driven by higher financial deficits at the state and municipal level and social security system.

Structural Fiscal Challenges: A new Austrian Stability Pact was finalised on 28 November 2025 to strengthen fiscal transparency and improve fiscal coordination across all government levels (federal, state and municipal). The improved fiscal framework sets out a commitment to fiscal discipline, but Fitch believes reforms will be needed to address rising healthcare, education and social services expenditure pressures, and meet fiscal targets. Challenges from an ageing demographic and limited progress in structural reform make consolidation of these expenditures difficult.

Increasing Government Debt Ratio: Our fiscal projections place general government debt on a gradually upward trajectory. Headline fiscal deficits above 4.0% of GDP through 2027 will raise Austria's debt-to-GDP ratio to about 84.8% at end-2027, up from an estimated 82.1% at end-2025. Austria's debt ratio is nearly twice that of 'AA' rated sovereigns (historical median ratio at 43.6% of GDP).

Favourable Debt Structure: The government benefits from low financing costs and a long average debt maturity (about 13 years). Nevertheless, interest expenditure is set to increase from 1.5% of GDP in 2024 to 1.8% in 2027, according to Fitch's estimates.

Strong Private External Position: Austria has a positive net international investment position (IIP) at around 22.9% of GDP at end-2024, reflecting the strong net creditor position of the private sector. Net external debt of 13.9% of GDP (at end-2024) largely reflects the sovereign's negative net foreign asset position (excluding TARGET2 net liabilities at 16.3% of GDP).

Current Account Surpluses: A well-diversified external sector in goods and services underpin persistent current account surpluses (except in 2022 due to Europe's energy shock and Austria's large dependency on gas imports). We forecast Austria's current account will average 1.8% of GDP in 2026-2027.

Gradual Economic Recovery: Austria is gradually recovering after two years of recession. Fitch forecasts real GDP growth of 0.3% in 2025, 1.0% in 2026 and 1.5% in 2027. Lower inflation will support real wage growth, and we expect some unwinding of household savings. Meanwhile, a gradual recovery in business sentiment in the construction and manufacturing sectors should aid investment growth, which in real terms has not yet returned to its 2022 level.

Structural Growth Challenges Ahead: Austria's cost competitiveness has weakened in recent years due to high energy and labour costs, hitting export-orientated manufacturing hardest. The sector faces weak productivity growth, energy transition expenses, and rising global competition, notably from China. Medium-term GDP potential is estimated by the European Commission at 0.8%, below the eurozone average of 1.2%.

Banking Sector CRE Risks: The Austrian banking sector has a robust capital position and has demonstrated resilience to a challenging macroeconomic backdrop. However, the commercial real estate (CRE) sector remains a financial vulnerability. Banks' CRE exposure (about 15%-20% of the domestic corporate loan book) is high by EU standards, and asset quality has deteriorated. The CRE non-performing loan (NPL) ratio rose from about 1.5% in mid2023 to about 7.9% in mid2025, prompting the financial authority to impose a 1% systemic risk buffer in July 2025. Deterioration has slowed. Fitch expects a lengthy and slow recovery of the sector.

ESG - Governance: Austria has an ESG Relevance Score (RS) of '5[+]' for both Political Stability and Rights and for the Rule of Law, Institutional and Regulatory Quality and Control of Corruption. These scores reflect the high weight that the World Banking Governance Indicators (WBGI) have in our proprietary Sovereign Rating Model (SRM). Austria has a high WBGI ranking at 87.6, reflecting its long record of stable and peaceful political transitions, well established rights for participation in the political process, strong institutional capacity, effective rule of law and a low level of corruption.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

-Public Finances: A further substantial increase of government debt-to-GDP ratio beyond our current forecasts, for example, due to failure to implement fiscal consolidation measures or weak revenue dynamics.

-Macro: A persistent weak growth outlook, for example, due to weaker potential growth or an erosion of external competitiveness.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

-Public Finances: General government debt-to-GDP ratio on a clear downward path over the medium term to significantly lower levels, due to stronger economic growth or fiscal consolidation.

Sovereign Rating Model (SRM) and Qualitative Overlay (QO)

Fitch's proprietary SRM assigns Austria a score equivalent to a rating of 'AA-' on the Long-Term Foreign-Currency (LT FC) IDR scale.

Fitch's sovereign rating committee adjusted the output from the SRM to arrive at the final LT FC IDR by applying its QO, relative to SRM data and output, as follows:

- Macro: +1 notch to reflect the deterioration of the GDP volatility variable caused by a very substantial exogenous shock that hit the vast majority of sovereigns in 2020. Fitch believes that Austria has absorbed the shock without lasting effects on its long-term macroeconomic stability, and that the shock would otherwise add excess volatility to the rating.

Fitch's SRM is the agency's proprietary multiple regression rating model that employs 18 variables based on three-year centred averages, including one year of forecasts, to produce a score equivalent to a LT FC IDR. Fitch's QO is a forward-looking qualitative framework designed to allow for adjustment to the SRM output to assign the final rating, reflecting factors within our criteria that are not fully quantifiable and/or not fully reflected in the SRM.

Debt Instruments: Key Rating Drivers

Senior Unsecured Debt Equalised: The senior unsecured long-term debt ratings are equalised with the applicable Long-Term IDR, as Fitch assumes recoveries will be 'average' when the sovereign's Long-Term IDR is 'BB-' and above. No Recovery Ratings are assigned at this rating level.

Country Ceiling

The Country Ceiling for Austria is 'AAA', two notches above the LT FC IDR. This reflects very strong constraints and incentives, relative to the IDR, against capital or exchange controls being imposed that would prevent or significantly impede the private sector from converting LC into FC and transferring the proceeds to non-resident creditors to service debt payments.

Fitch's Country Ceiling Model produced a starting point uplift of +3 notches above the IDR. Fitch's rating committee did not apply a qualitative adjustment to the model result.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. Climate Vulnerability Signals

The results of our Climate.VS screener did not indicate an elevated risk for Austria

ESG Considerations

Austria has an ESG Relevance Score of '5[+]' for Political Stability and Rights as WBGI have the highest weight in Fitch's SRM and are therefore highly relevant to the rating and a key rating driver with a high weight. As Austria has a percentile rank above 50 for the respective Governance Indicator, this has a positive impact on the credit profile.

Austria has an ESG Relevance Score of '5[+]' for Rule of Law, Institutional & Regulatory Quality and Control of Corruption as WBGI have the highest weight in Fitch's SRM and are therefore highly relevant to the rating and are a key rating driver with a high weight. As Austria has a percentile rank above 50 for the respective Governance Indicators, this has a positive impact on the credit profile.

Austria has an ESG Relevance Score of '4[+]' for Human Rights and Political Freedoms as the Voice and Accountability pillar of the WBGI is relevant to the rating and a rating driver. As Austria has a percentile rank above 50 for the respective Governance Indicator, this has a positive impact on the credit profile.

Austria has an ESG Relevance Score of '4[+]' for Creditor Rights as willingness to service and repay debt is relevant to the rating and is a rating driver for Austria, as for all sovereigns. As Austria has a record of 20+ years without a restructuring of public debt and captured in our SRM variable, this has a positive impact on the credit profile.

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

data.title: PLATTS: 100--Energy industry group commits to solving fusion energy supply chain challenges
data.description: (Platts)--05Dec2025/502 pm EST/2202 GMT **Fusion energy faces supply chain risks **Policies needed to support nascent industry The nonprofit US Energy Association is committed to working with policymakers and industry to improve supply chains and promote fusion energy development. "This is a national security and economic competitiveness issue," USEA President Mark Menezes said in a Dec. 4 news release. "[The US] must prioritize strategic investment in domestic mining, processing and manufacturing capabilities, especially for enriched lithium and high-purity components." Menezes called on the federal government, the private sector and research institutions to partner in building the resilience needed "to unlock fusion's immense potential and secure America's energy future." He said the industry is on track to begin producing power by the 2030s, and policy must focus on de-risking the supply chain. Menezes was responding to the US Fusion Supply Chain Report, released by the Special Competitive Studies Project on Dec. 3. "Supply chain challenges could stall a burgeoning fusion industry, and addressing them effectively will be vital for the industry to rapidly grow," the SCSP report said. The SCSP is a bipartisan policy research group founded by former Google CEO Eric Schmidt. The SCSP report provided recommendations on ensuring that the US supports the industry's raw material and component needs so as not to become dependent on China, which is a leading producer of items that US fusion companies need. Menezes had been a member of SCSP's Commission on the Scaling of Fusion Energy, and he had invited the fusion industry trade association to address USEA's annual member and policy meeting in May. USEA spokesperson Melissa Brown said in a Dec. 5 email that the association "supports all forms of energy and has members in the fusion field." "It's entirely appropriate for the association of all of US energy to be out there in support of the next big energy source," Andrew Holland, president of the Fusion Industry Association, told Platts, part of S&P Global Energy. Holland noted Menezes' prior work with SCSP. It is urgent that the US act to secure vital components, materials and manufacturing capacity for the new industry, as it plans to bring reactors online in as little as three years, the SCSP said in the supply chain report. A top issue of concern raised by the report is ensuring "domestic and friendly nation production of maximally threatened raw materials." These critical minerals include gallium, germanium, gadolinium and yttrium. It also calls for the creation of a US supply of enriched lithium-6, which would be used to make tritium, the primary fuel for a fusion power plant. This would involve establishing more flexible trading relations with Canada, which looks to be the world's top tritium producer until domestic production can be ramped up, the report said. "The United States should ensure continued flexible trade [with Canada] to maintain access to the tritium supplies that will be needed for fusion-plant startup," the report added. TRADE TALKS As of Sept. 1, the US allows most Canadian goods to enter the US tariff-free under the Canada-United States-Mexico Agreement. In response, Canada has removed most counter tariffs on US goods put in place in March. The Canadian government said counter tariffs remain on steel, aluminum and automobiles amid "intensive" negotiations with the US administration to resolve tariff disputes. Adding exceptions for fusion components in trade negotiations would help the industry, the report said. "In the near and medium term, strategically partnering with like-minded nations like the United Kingdom, Japan and Germany and including specialized fusion components like cryogenics and gyrotrons in trade deals and tariff exemptions would help stave off potential chokepoints," the report said. "Recent bilateral tech partnerships with the UK and Japan have already begun this process." TAX POLICY The report also called on Congress to update tax policy to help attract investment in domestic fusion component manufacturing. This would include updating the 45X and 48C tax subsidies to include fusion components "to encourage domestic development of manufactured parts." Legislation was introduced during the government shutdown in both the House and Senate to include fusion components under the 45X advanced manufacturing tax credit program. The Fusion Advanced Manufacturing Parity Act is expected to be debated in Congress in 2026. The fusion industry is actively supporting the bill, but Holland said in a Dec. 5 email that he has not heard when the Senate Finance Committee or the House Ways and Means Committee plan to take up the measure. Menezes said USEA is committed "to working with policymakers and industry stakeholders to translate the report's recommendations into actionable strategies to strengthen the domestic fusion energy ecosystem." Brown said the association hopes to hold either an event or briefing in the spring to address the future of fusion energy and the SCSP's work. — John Siciliano, john.siciliano@spglobal.com-- Edited by Lyle Crouse, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: DOCUSIGN, INC. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
DocuSign, Inc., a leading provider of electronic signature and agreement cloud solutions, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter, showcasing robust financial and operational performance. The company continues to expand its footprint in the commercial and enterprise sectors, driven by innovative product offerings and strategic investments.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenue: $818.4 million, an 8% increase compared to the same period last year, driven by expansion in commercial and enterprise accounts.
  • Gross Profit: $647.8 million, representing a gross margin of 79%, consistent with the prior year.
  • Income from Operations: $85.4 million, reflecting a 10% operating margin, up from 8% in the previous year.
  • Net Income: $83.7 million, an increase from $62.4 million in the prior year, attributed to higher revenue and operational efficiency.
  • Net Income Per Share (Basic): $0.41, compared to $0.31 in the previous year, reflecting improved profitability.
  • Net Income Per Share (Diluted): $0.40, compared to $0.30 in the previous year, indicating enhanced earnings performance.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: Subscription revenue accounted for 98% of total revenue, driven by expansion in commercial and enterprise accounts, as well as digital channels. Professional services and other revenue made up the remaining 2%, with a focus on deployment and integration services.
  • Geographical Performance: International revenue increased by 13% year-over-year, representing 30% of total revenue in the latest quarter. The company has made significant investments in select civil law countries, offering Standards-Based Signature technology tailored for EU regulations.
  • Customer Base Growth: The company reported nearly 1.8 million total customers, including approximately 276,000 SMBs, mid-market, and large enterprise customers served by the direct sales force. The number of customers with greater than $300,000 in annualized contract value increased to 1,165.
  • Sales Channels: The company is investing in three routes to market: direct sales, partner-assisted sales, and digital self-service purchasing, with the IAM platform increasingly offered across all channels.
  • Product Innovation: Continued investment in research and development to enhance the IAM platform, including the acquisition of Lexion to support product innovation.
  • Operational Efficiency: The company is focusing on enhancing operational and financial efficiency to scale effectively, prioritizing infrastructure and technology investments that best serve a diverse customer base.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue investing in long-term growth through product innovation, strengthening omnichannel go-to-market strategies, and enhancing operational efficiency. Strategic acquisitions and partnerships are also being evaluated to expand product offerings.

data.title: BEST BUY CO INC SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Best Buy Co Inc, a leading provider of consumer electronics, computing, and mobile phone products, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial and operational performance, highlighting key metrics and future outlooks.

Financial Highlights

Revenue: Best Buy reported revenue of $9,672 million, marking a 2.4% increase compared to the same period last year. This growth was primarily driven by strong performance in computing, gaming, and mobile phones.

Gross Profit: The company achieved a gross profit of $2,248 million, which represents 23.2% of revenue. This is a slight decrease from 23.5% in the previous year, attributed to lower product margin rates.

Operating Income: Operating income for the quarter was $198 million, down from $350 million in the prior year. The decline was mainly due to goodwill and intangible asset impairments.

Net Earnings: Net earnings stood at $140 million, a decrease from $273 million in the previous year, impacted by higher impairment charges.

Diluted EPS: Diluted earnings per share (EPS) were $0.66, down from $1.26 last year, reflecting lower net earnings and higher impairment charges.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The Domestic segment experienced growth in computing, gaming, and mobile phones, while home theater and appliances saw declines. The International segment also reported growth in computing and mobile phones, with additional revenue from Best Buy Express locations in Canada.

Geographical Performance: The Domestic segment's revenue increased by 2.1% in the third quarter, driven by strong performance in computing and mobile phones. The International segment's revenue grew by 6.1%, with notable growth in computing and mobile phones, despite the negative impact of foreign exchange rates.

Sales Units: The Domestic segment's online revenue increased by 3.5% in the third quarter, accounting for 31.8% of total segment revenue. The International segment reported a 6.3% increase in comparable sales, driven by computing and mobile phones.

Future Outlook: Best Buy plans to continue optimizing its store count and expects to close additional non-traditional Domestic store locations in fiscal 2027 as part of its restructuring initiatives. The company is also focused on enhancing its Best Buy Health business to improve profitability.

data.title: Fitch Affirms City of Almaty at 'BBB'; Outlook Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Dubai-05 December 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed the City of Almaty's Long-Term Issuer Default Ratings (IDR) at 'BBB' with Stable Outlook. A full list of rating actions is below.

The affirmation reflects Fitch's unchanged assessment of Almaty's Standalone Credit Profile (SCP) at 'bbb+'. This reflects a 'Weaker' risk profile and a financial profile assessed at 'aaa', based on our expectations that Almaty's payback ratio will stay below 5x in 2029 under our rating-case scenario. The city's IDRs are one notch below its SCP, as they are capped by Kazakhstan's sovereign IDR (BBB/Stable).

KEY RATING DRIVERS Standalone Credit Profile

The 'bbb+' SCP reflects the combination of a 'Weaker' risk profile and a financial profile in the higher end of the 'aaa' category.

Risk Profile::'Weaker'

Almaty's 'Weaker' risk profile reflects the combination of 'Weaker' and 'Midrange' assessments of six key risk factors, as outlined below.

Revenue Robustness::'Midrange'

Almaty's revenue items remained sound in 2022-2024 and on an interim basis in 2025. Taxes remain the prime source of the city's revenue base. The city collects personal income tax and receives proceeds from corporate income tax collected from profits of small and some medium-sized enterprises. Income taxes comprised about 65% of total revenue in 2024.

Revenue Adjustability::'Weaker'

Like other cities and regions in Kazakhstan, Almaty has no rate-setting power over taxes as the central government has full fiscal authority over rates and the amount of revenue allocated between levels of government. Additional revenue from a tax increase would be low compared with any reasonable decline in revenue we could expect in an economic downturn. This limits our assessment of Almaty's fiscal autonomy and revenue adjustability.

Expenditure Sustainability::'Midrange'

Non-cyclical or moderately cyclical items dominate Almaty's expenditure. Sectors less prone to cyclicality, such as education, healthcare, utilities and transport, made up about 62% of total expenditure in 2024. The city is not required to adopt anti-cyclical measures, which could inflate social spending in a downturn.

Expenditure Adjustability::'Weaker'

Most of Almaty's opex is mandatory, which makes it difficult to curtail if revenue contracts. As a net donor to Kazakhstan's fiscal system, Almaty makes compulsory annual transfers to the state budget, with amounts determined by the central government. Historically, these have been about 20% of total expenditure. In 2023-2024, they decreased to 14% of Almaty's spending.

Liabilities and Liquidity Robustness::'Midrange'

Local and regional governments (LRG) in Kazakhstan are subject to strict debt-management regulation, including restrictions on debt stock, types of borrowing and limits on annual interest payments. The central government sets annual limits on LRGs' debt volume. Subsidised budget loans are the only form of debt available for most LRGs in Kazakhstan. However, due to its special republic status, Almaty can issue bonds.

LRGs are banned from issuing guarantees, so government-related entities' debt is the only form of contingent liabilities for the city. At end-2024, several municipal companies had debt on their balance sheets, which is all state-related and self-serviced.

Liabilities and Liquidity Flexibility::'Weaker'

The state regulatory body sets limits on the liquidity policy of local governments. LRGs cannot place excess liquidity with commercial banks, but can deposit cash reserves with the National Bank of Kazakhstan. The term of deposits cannot exceed one week while the annual interest rate is far below the terms offered on the market. There is no formal framework in place providing emergency liquidity mechanism from the state to the local governments in Kazakhstan, and Almaty does not have committed liquidity lines with banks.

Financial Profile::'aaa category'

Fitch assesses Almaty's financial profile in the 'aaa' category. This reflects strong primary (payback ratio below 2x) and secondary metrics (fiscal debt burden below 10%) that Fitch assesses at 'aaa' in its rating-case scenario for 2025-2029.

We expect Almaty's operating balance to slightly increase in 2025-2029, averaging KZT508 billion (average 2020-2024: KZT453 billion) in our rating case. The city's operating balance is primarily supported by revenue from taxes, which is projected to rise to KZT2.8 trillion by 2029, driven by expected growth in the local economy. This is balanced by a projected increase in opex, underpinned by continued inflationary pressure over the rating period.

Almaty's capex increased to KZT555 billion in 2024 (up 7% compared with 2023). We expect the city to continue implementation of its capex with projected annual capital investments averaging KZT723 billion in 2025-2029, mostly for modernisation and expansion of the city's infrastructure.

Other Rating Factors

Almaty's Long-Term IDR is capped by the sovereign. Kazakh LRGs may not be rated above the state, reflecting the latter's influence on its LRGs' duties and finances.

Short-Term Ratings

Almaty's 'F2' Short-Term IDR reflects a 'Midrange' assessment of the liabilities and liquidity robustness and liquidity coverage ratio at above 1.3x in our rating case.

National Ratings

The National Rating of 'AAA(kaz)' maps to a 'BBB' Long-Term IDR for Kazakh issuers.

Peer Analysis

The city compares well with the City of Yerevan (BB-/Stable) and Istanbul Metropolitan Municipality (BB-/Stable), which both have 'bbb-' SCPs and payback ratios below 5x under our rating case, resulting in a 'aaa' financial profile. Both entities, like Almaty, remained capped by the IDR of their respective sovereign, Armenia (BB-/Stable) and Turkiye (BB-/Stable).

Issuer Profile

Almaty, the largest city in Kazakhstan, is an important economic and financial centre and contributes about 20% to the country's GDP. The city's budget accounts are cash-based; the city budget law lasts for three years.

Key Assumptions

Qualitative assumptions:

Risk Profile::'Weaker' Revenue Robustness::'Midrange' Revenue Adjustability::'Weaker' Expenditure Sustainability::'Midrange' Expenditure Adjustability::'Weaker' Liabilities and Liquidity Robustness::'Midrange' Liabilities and Liquidity Flexibility::'Weaker' Financial Profile::'aaa' Asymmetric Risk::'N/A' Support (Budget Loans)::'N/A' Support (Ad Hoc)::'N/A' Rating Cap (LT IDR)::'BBB' Rating Cap (LT LC IDR):'BBB' Rating Floor::'N/A' Quantitative assumptions - Issuer Specific

The rating action is driven by the following assumptions for reference metrics under our 2025-2029 rating case:

- Payback ratio: maximum of 1.8x in 2029

- Actual debt service coverage ratio: minimum of 3.1x in 2029

- Fiscal debt burden: below 10% in 2025-2029

Fitch's through-the-cycle rating case incorporates a combination of revenue, cost and financial risk stresses. It is based on 2020-2024 published figures and our expectations for 2025-2029:

- Operating revenue growth on average at 9% a year, markedly below the historical increase of 30% a year in 2020-2024, due to added stress

- Opex growth on average at 20.7% a year, underpinned by projected inflation over 2025-2029 and added stress

- Negative net capital balance on average at KZT639 billion a year, sustaining the city's large capex programme

- Average 6.5% cost of debt (2024: 3.7%) due to added stress

Rating Sensitivities Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

Deterioration of debt payback towards 5x on a sustained basis under Fitch's rating case could lead to a downgrade of Almaty's ratings, as would negative rating action on the sovereign.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

An upgrade of the sovereign rating, provided Almaty's SCP remains 'bbb+', could lead to an upgrade.

ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit-neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit https://www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

Discussion Note

Committee date: 02 December 2025

There was an appropriate quorum at the committee and the members confirmed that they were free from recusal. It was agreed that the data was sufficiently robust relative to its materiality. During the committee no material issues were raised that were not in the original committee package. The main rating factors under the relevant criteria were discussed by the committee members. The rating decision as discussed in this rating action commentary reflects the committee discussion.

Public Ratings with Credit Linkage to other ratings

The city's ratings are capped by those of Kazakhstan

data.title: Fitch Affirms French Department of Essonne at 'A+'; Outlook Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Paris-05 December 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed the Department of Essonne's Long-Term Foreign- and Local-Currency Issuer Default Ratings (IDRs) at 'A+', with Stable Outlooks. A full list of rating actions is below.

The affirmation reflects Fitch's unchanged assessment of Essonne's Standalone Credit Profile (SCP) at 'aa-'. The IDRs are capped by France's sovereign rating (A+/Stable).

KEY RATING DRIVERS Standalone Credit Profile

Essonne's 'aa-' SCP reflects the combination of a 'High Midrange' risk profile and financial profile assessed at the lower end of the 'aa' category with a payback (net adjusted debt/operating balance) slightly below 8x in 2028-2029 in our rating case.

Risk Profile::'High Midrange'

Essonne's 'High Midrange' risk profile reflects the combination of 'Stronger' and 'Midrange' assessments of six key risk factors, as outlined below.

Revenue Robustness::'Stronger'

Essonne benefits from robust revenue, comprising expanding and predictable tax items, such as that on insurance contracts and VAT (14% and 36% of operating revenue in 2024, respectively). The risk of a sustained decline in VAT proceeds is low, as they are based on national consumption and linked to nominal GDP rises over the long term. The department also receives a large share of stable transfers and fuel tax revenue, the proceeds of which are mostly guaranteed by the state (together 26%) with moderate counterparty risk. Property transfer duties (13%) represent the main source of budget volatility as they are linked to real-estate transactions.

Revenue Adjustability::'Midrange'

French departments benefit from a cost pass-through mechanism, with the state as their counterparty. They are responsible for public spending that is decided nationally, especially social spending, and tend to receive greater financial support from France when costs increase.

Expenditure Sustainability::'Midrange'

A large share of Essonne's expenditure depends on national decisions, limiting the department's ability to control them, including social spending. Social benefits are a counter-cyclical item as they tend to go up when unemployment rises (17% of opex in 2024). Essonne's opex has increased faster than revenue over the past five years - on average at 2.4% a year versus 1.7% - mostly linked to a sharp fall in property transfer duties and exceptionally high inflation in 2023.

Expenditure Adjustability::'Midrange'

Essonne's opex flexibility is limited and mainly comprises mandatory current transfers, including social spending and social accommodation fees (56% of opex in 2024). Staff costs (18%) are also a rigid spending item as most of the department's employees have the status of civil servants. Nevertheless, Essonne has managed to keep staff costs mostly stable from 2023 despite inflation, underlining the department's strict cost control policy. The department has more flexibility over capex through postponement or cutbacks. We estimate the share of non-flexible items at between 70% and 90% of total spending.

Liabilities and Liquidity Robustness::'Stronger'

Essonne's liabilities carry little risk. At end-2024, 57% was fixed rate and 100% was considered risk free under national regulation (Gissler Charter). Debt service remains structurally covered by the department's operating balance (2024: 1.1x). Off-balance sheet liabilities (guarantees plus public-sector entities' debt) were mostly flat in 2024 and represented 3x the department's operating balance. About a third of the guarantees were related to the low-risk social housing sector, while the rest was linked to socio-medical sectors.

Liabilities and Liquidity Flexibility::'Midrange'

The framework for emergency liquidity support from the state is 'Midrange', given the sovereign's rating in the 'A' category. This is underlined by cash-pooling between French local and regional governments and the state, with the former's liquidity deposited at the Treasury. French local and regional governments may benefit from tax proceeds in advance, limiting the risk of a liquidity shortfall. The department also has access to institutional lenders, especially the Caisse des Depots et Consignations (A+/Stable), and commercial banks rated in the 'A' category.

Financial Profile::'aa category'

We assess Essonne's financial profile at the lower end of the 'aa' category. Our rating case expects its payback ratio to be slightly below 8x in 2028-2029 (2024: 8.4x), the debt service coverage (Fitch's synthetic calculation) to be about 1.6x, and the fiscal debt burden at below 75%.

The operating balance should recover throughout our rating case to about EUR150 million in 2029 (EUR121 million in 2024), driven by a rebound in property transfer duties, the implementation of a savings programme on voluntary costs and a gradual slowdown in social spending growth.

Essonne's net capex programme has been revised down greatly, to about EUR150 million a year in our rating case, compared with EUR190 million a year over the past five years. This should limit the rise in its net adjusted debt, which we expect to reach EUR1.2 billion by end-2029, from EUR1 billion at end-2024.

Other Rating Factors

The ratings of Essonne are capped by those of France because of the strong influence the state exercises on the responsibilities and decisions taken by local and regional governments. No other factors affect the ratings.

Short-Term Ratings

The 'F1+' Short-Term IDR reflects 'Stronger' liabilities and liquidity robustness, 'Midrange' liabilities and liquidity flexibility, and liquidity coverage above 1.8x in 2025 and 2026 under our rating case.

Debt Ratings

Essonne's EUR1 billion EMTN programme, EUR180 million NeuCP programme and senior unsecured issues are rated in line with its IDRs.

Peer Analysis

Essonne compares well with other departments from Metropolitan France, which all have the same 'A+' ratings due to the sovereign cap. Essonne's SCP is lower than that of Val d'Oise (aa) or Yvelines (aa+) but one notch above that of Seine Saint Denis (a+), reflecting differences in their financial profiles.

Internationally, French departments compare well with New Zealand's councils, such as Rotorua Lakes Council (AA-/Stable), which shares the same risk profile and SCP as Essonne. The former's IDRs are higher, however, because the sovereign cap for New Zealand (AA+/Stable) is higher than for France.

Issuer Profile

Essonne is in the Region of Ile-de-France (A+/Stable). Like other French departments, its main responsibilities cover social services, road maintenance and secondary education.

Key Assumptions Risk Profile::'High Midrange' Revenue Robustness::'Stronger' Revenue Adjustability::'Midrange' Expenditure Sustainability::'Midrange' Expenditure Adjustability::'Midrange' Liabilities and Liquidity Robustness::'Stronger' Liabilities and Liquidity Flexibility::'Midrange' Financial Profile::'aa' Asymmetric Risk::'N/A' Support (Budget Loans)::'N/A' Support (Ad Hoc)::'N/A' Rating Cap (LT IDR)::'A+' Rating Cap (LT LC IDR):'A+' Rating Floor::'N/A' Quantitative assumptions - Issuer Specific

Fitch's rating action is driven by the following assumptions for reference metrics under our 2025-2029 rating case:

- Payback ratio: 7.8x

- Synthetic coverage ratio: 1.6x

- Fiscal debt burden: 75%

Fitch's rating case is a "through-the-cycle" scenario, which is based on published 2020-2024 figures. The key assumptions for 2025-2029 include:

- Operating revenue growth on average at 2.4% a year, due to a rebound in property transfer duties from 2025

- Opex growth on average at 2.2% a year, mostly driven by social spending growth partly offset by a large-scale savings plan

- Negative net capital balance on average at EUR154 million a year, in line with the department's capex plan

- Average 2.4% cost of debt (including 3.4% cost of new borrowing)

We have not taken into account the 2026 Draft Budget Law measures in our scenario as they are subject to change.

Rating Sensitivities Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

Deterioration of the payback to above 10.5x on a sustained basis in our rating case could lead to a downgrade.

A downgrade of the sovereign would also be reflected in Essonne's ratings.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

An upgrade of France would be reflected in Essonne's ratings, all else being equal.

ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. data.title: Netflix's Megadeal Will Need the Trump Administration's Blessing — WSJ
data.description:
By Joe Flint, Dave Michaels and Lauren Thomas

Netflix beat out Paramount and Comcast to land a $72 billion deal for Warner Bros. Discovery's studios and HBO Max streaming service.

Now it has to win over the Trump administration.

The streaming giant's deal is expected to be investigated by the Justice Department, which has already begun considering how it would further cement Netflix's dominance in the media industry.

It will also have to contend with the preferences of President Trump, who is close with Paramount Chief Executive David Ellison and his father and could pressure antitrust enforcers to steer Warner toward Paramount instead. A senior administration official said Friday that Trump advisers, including White House officials, are concerned about Netflix's deal.

Paramount, meanwhile, had in recent days accused Warner of unfairly favoring Netflix's deal, which it said would be frowned upon by regulators in the U.S. and abroad. Paramount, whose shares slumped Friday after Netflix prevailed, is now weighing its next move, people familiar with the matter said.

Netflix, the largest streamer, has said the deal for Warner's vast TV and movie libraries as well as HBO and its HBO Max streaming platform would help it attract and retain more subscribers. Netflix has also said it would keep Warner Bros. and HBO making shows and movies which is good for the creative community.

Netflix co-CEO Ted Sarandos said Friday the company is "highly confident" the deal will win approval because it is "pro-consumer" and "pro-innovation."

The Justice Department would have to formally investigate to determine whether it should challenge the deal. Merger investigations typically take at least 10 months before the government files suit to block the transaction.

More so than in previous administrations, the White House is likely to influence where regulators land, said Sam Weinstein, a professor at the Cardozo School of Law.

During Trump's first term, the Justice Department unsuccessfully challenged AT&T's acquisition of Time Warner after Trump had complained about Time Warner-owned CNN's coverage of him.

More recently, the Trump administration approved Skydance Media's merger with Paramount. Ellison, the son of Oracle co-founder Larry Ellison, a Trump ally, had promised the Federal Communications Commission that CBS News, a unit of Paramount, would deliver unbiased reporting and that Paramount's diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives would end.

Trump's current antitrust chiefs — Gail Slater at the Justice Department and Andrew Ferguson at the FTC — have had a relatively light touch so far when it comes to policing big deals. Both have made clear they are focused on protecting competition in cost-of-living sectors, such as healthcare and housing.

Many companies have gotten deals blessed by offering settlements, which typically involve agreeing to sell pieces of a business to a third party.

Some inside the Justice Department already believe Netflix's acquiring Warner's HBO Max might violate antitrust laws because of the combined entity's scale among streaming services, The Wall Street Journal previously reported.

Netflix and HBO Max together would control around 30% of the U.S. subscription streaming marketplace.

Mergers of direct competitors are presumed to be illegal when the combined company has over 30% market share, according to Justice Department guidelines announced in 2023 under former President Biden and retained by the Trump administration.

Netflix has argued that the streaming marketplace should also include mostly free video platforms such as YouTube, Facebook and TikTok. It also believes that even if the market is limited to subscription-based services, there is no evidence that adding HBO Max would reduce competition or harm consumers, according to a person familiar with the matter.

Netflix has said HBO and its streaming service HBO Max will continue to operate as stand-alone entities after the deal closes but will be both bundled and separately sold alongside Netflix, which could mean lower prices for consumers.

In a potential sign of Netflix's confidence, it agreed to pay Warner a so-called breakup fee of $5.8 billion if the deal was scuttled because the companies couldn't obtain necessary approvals.

That fee is one of the largest in history and represents 8% of the total deal value, well above the 1% to 3% range that is more typical.

Warner Discovery and Netflix have said they anticipate the deal to take 12 to 18 months to close.

Netflix hired lawyers at Skadden to guide it through the deal, including antitrust veteran Steven Sunshine.

Paramount hired its own legal heavyweight in Makan Delrahim, who ran the Justice Department's antitrust division during Trump's first term . He joined Paramount as chief legal officer in September.

Netflix's deal has already drawn scrutiny from lawmakers in both parties as well as in Hollywood.

U.S. Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D., Mass.) said Friday on X that the proposed deal is an "anti-monopoly nightmare" that will raise consumer costs and limit choice.

U.S. Sen. Mike Lee (R., Utah) earlier wrote on X that the potential deal "would raise serious competition questions — perhaps more so than any transaction I've seen in about a decade."

Lee leads the Senate Judiciary Subcommittee's antitrust subcommittee, which could hold hearings and force Netflix and Warner to answer questions about the deal.

The movie industry could also be radically altered by Netflix's taking over Warner Bros. Netflix has historically preferred to keep most of its films on its platform rather than give them theatrical releases. It has indicated it will maintain Warner Bros.' current operations including releasing movies in theaters, but some in the industry are dubious.

"The proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. by Netflix poses an unprecedented threat to the global exhibition business, said Michael O'Leary, president of movie industry lobbying arm Cinema United.

Write to Joe Flint at Joe.Flint@wsj.com, Dave Michaels at dave.michaels@wsj.com and Lauren Thomas at lauren.thomas@wsj.com

data.title: California Resources halts pipeline flow after leak at San Ardo Oil Field
data.description:
By Nicole Jao

California Resources on Friday morning reported a leak at San Ardo Oil Field in Monterey County, California, according to a filing with a state regulator.

The flow to the pipe has been halted and the discharge has been stopped, California Resources said in the filing with California Governor's Office Emergency Service on Friday.

A release of 93 barrels of produced water and 3 barrels of oil was found coming from a group line pipe, according to the filing. No waterways or storm drains were impacted.

A crew was on site working on repairs to the pipe. No injuries or evacuations were reported.

California Resources did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Heavy oil produced at San Ardo supplies to the Los Angeles refining market, the company's website shows.

data.title: Planet Fitness Prices $750 Million Securitized Financing Facility
data.description:
HAMPTON, N.H., Dec. 5, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Planet Fitness, Inc. (together with its subsidiaries, the "Company") today announced that it has priced $750 million of Series 2025-1 Class A-2 Fixed Rate Senior Secured Notes (the "Class A-2 Notes"), which consist of two tranches: the Class A-2-I Senior Secured Notes with an anticipated repayment term of five years, with an aggregate principal amount of $400 million and a fixed interest rate of 5.274% per annum, payable quarterly, and the Class A-2-II Senior Secured Notes with an anticipated repayment term of seven years, with an aggregate principal amount of $350 million and a fixed interest rate of 5.649% per annum, payable quarterly. The Class A-2 Notes are expected to be issued by Planet Fitness Master Issuer LLC (the "Master Issuer"), a limited-purpose, bankruptcy remote, indirect subsidiary of Planet Fitness, Inc. in a privately placed securitization transaction. In addition to the Class A-2 Notes, the Company expects that it will enter into a $75 million variable funding note facility, in addition to the existing $75 million 2022-1 Variable Funding Senior Secured Notes, Class A-1 (the "Class A-1 Notes"). As of September 30, 2025, there were no borrowings outstanding under the existing variable funding notes facility.

The proceeds from the expected sale of the Class A-2 Notes will be used as follows:

  • to repay in full the Series 2022-1 Class A-2-I Notes, which as of September 30, 2025, had a principal balance of approximately $410 million;
  • to pay the transaction costs and fund the reserve accounts associated with the securitized financing facility; and
  • for general corporate purposes, which may include funding share repurchases by the Company.

The Company expects the Class A-1 Notes and the Class A-2 Notes transaction to close on or around December 15, 2025, subject to satisfaction of various closing conditions. There can be no assurance regarding the timing of closing or that the sale of the Class A-2 Notes and the Class A-1 Notes will be completed.

This press release does not constitute an offer to sell or the solicitation of an offer to buy the Class A-1 Notes, the Class A-2 Notes or any other security. The Class A-2 Notes to be offered have not been, and will not be, registered under the Securities Act of 1933 and may not be offered or sold in the United States absent registration or an applicable exemption from the registration requirements of the Securities Act of 1933.

About Planet Fitness

Founded in 1992 in Dover, NH, Planet Fitness is one of the largest and fastest-growing franchisors and operators of fitness centers in the world by number of members and locations. As of September 30, 2025, Planet Fitness had approximately 20.7 million members and 2,795 clubs in all 50 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, Canada, Panama, Mexico, Australia and Spain. The Company's mission is to enhance people's lives by providing a high-quality fitness experience in a welcoming, non-intimidating environment, which we call the Judgement Free Zone(R). More than 90% of Planet Fitness clubs are owned and operated by independent business men and women.
